rationale: `src/fetch.js` does `import axios from 'axios'` and `import { format } from 'date-fns'` but `package.json`'s `dependencies` object is empty. The `node_deps_completeness` layer walks the JS source, extracts every bare-specifier import, normalises scoped packages and subpath imports, and reports anything not declared in any of dependencies / devDependencies / peerDependencies / optionalDependencies. axios + date-fns both surface as missing. This is a STATIC layer (milliseconds), no subprocess — `npm install` would also fail eventually, but only AFTER spinning up a network round-trip; this case demonstrates that catching missing-dep statically gives the rework loop a clean signal long before install.

baseline_comparison.eslint: PARTIAL — eslint-plugin-import with the `no-extraneous-dependencies` rule catches this; vanilla eslint does not. Most teams don't enable the rule by default.
baseline_comparison.tsc: N/A — no tsconfig; this is plain JS.
baseline_comparison.npm_install: Eventually catches (no axios in node_modules → module-not-found at runtime). But install must run, which adds seconds and depends on network. Catching upstream is cheaper.
baseline_comparison.raw_llm_critique: UNRELIABLE — may or may not flag the missing deps depending on prompt framing.
baseline_comparison.static_tools: Most static tools require an eslint plugin specifically configured for this. Aegis enables the equivalent check by default.
